decision: Which current external mechanisms should Shipkit adopt, adapt, integrate, defer, or reject?
scope: Primary-source review of adjacent coding-agent, durable-workflow, research, sandbox, interoperability, policy, provenance, telemetry, and evaluation systems. This is architecture evidence, not market or user validation.

systems.name: Temporal
systems.mechanism: Crash-proof execution, replayable history, deterministic workflows, and idempotent activities.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Keep Shipkit's local inspectable cycle kernel; use Temporal only as a future optional distributed activity backend.
systems.limitation: Temporal proves workflow recovery, not repository correctness, product value, or Shipkit policy acceptance.
systems.falsificationTest: A future Temporal adapter must replay to the same legal Shipkit cycle state as the local backend without duplicate accepted actions.

systems.name: LangGraph
systems.mechanism: Durable checkpointers, thread-addressed state, interrupts, and resumable human input.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Treat a graph as an execution client or activity; Shipkit remains authoritative for policy, evidence, and accepted stage changes.
systems.limitation: On resume, the interrupted node restarts from its beginning, so pre-interrupt side effects must be idempotent.
systems.falsificationTest: A graph client and plain CLI client must consume identical Shipkit state and produce equivalent policy decisions.

systems.name: Pydantic AI durable execution
systems.mechanism: A provider-neutral durable execution layer with integrations for Temporal, DBOS, Prefect, and Restate.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Preserve portable Shipkit records and make any workflow-engine integration optional rather than growing a competing distributed runtime.
systems.limitation: Provider support does not define Shipkit's product-governance semantics or prove a backend is appropriate for local-first users.
systems.falsificationTest: Integrate only if a real multi-process use case cannot meet its recovery target with the local kernel and the adapter preserves all Shipkit invariants.

systems.name: OpenHands
systems.mechanism: Model-neutral coding-agent runtime, client-server sandbox execution, reusable workflows, policy, cost, and audit controls.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Use OpenHands as a bounded execution adapter; differentiate Shipkit through decision-to-outcome evidence rather than the generic control-plane label.
systems.limitation: Control-plane, scale, safety, and outcome statements are first-party product claims; the enterprise control plane now overlaps Shipkit's earlier positioning.
systems.falsificationTest: One approved Shipkit handoff must execute through OpenHands while Shipkit alone authorizes scope and accepts evidence and final verdicts.

systems.name: OpenAI Codex
systems.mechanism: Local coding-agent execution with sandbox permissions, repository instructions, skills, hooks, and context-isolated specialist agents.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Consume a parameter-bound execution handoff through a subprocess adapter; never store Codex sessions as authoritative cycle state.
systems.limitation: Parallel write-heavy agent work adds coordination risk, and agent permissions do not replace Shipkit's independent policy and evidence rules.
systems.falsificationTest: Interrupt Codex and resume the same Shipkit cycle with another agent without losing accepted state or widening action scope.

systems.name: GitHub Copilot hooks
systems.mechanism: Deterministic lifecycle hooks can inspect tool calls, deny or modify them, and emit auditable progress outside model reasoning.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Adapt the enforcement-point pattern while keeping Shipkit policy deterministic and independent of a particular agent runtime.
systems.limitation: Command-hook crashes fail closed but hook timeouts fail open, so hooks alone are not a complete security boundary.
systems.falsificationTest: Policy fixtures must produce equivalent allow or deny results through a hook adapter and the local Shipkit enforcement path, including timeout cases.

systems.name: mini-SWE-agent
systems.mechanism: A deliberately small agent loop and environment abstraction provide a baseline against unnecessary orchestration complexity.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Require new execution-layer complexity to beat a minimal command baseline on a defined protected metric.
systems.limitation: Issue-resolution performance does not establish product discovery, governance, release safety, or user value.
systems.falsificationTest: Reject an orchestration feature that does not improve success, cost, duration, safety, or portability over the minimal baseline.

systems.name: Daytona
systems.mechanism: Programmatic isolated computers with dedicated kernel, filesystem, network stack, resource limits, snapshots, pause, resume, and fork capabilities.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Keep authorization and evidence in Shipkit while a capability-negotiated provider owns remote containment and lifecycle.
systems.limitation: The hosted API requires credentials and creates cost, data-location, availability, and provider-trust decisions that need explicit approval.
systems.falsificationTest: Pilot only after hostile fixtures prove host isolation, secret denial, network policy, cleanup, and equivalent evidence capture.

systems.name: Model Context Protocol
systems.mechanism: Standard client-to-server transport for tools, resources, prompts, authorization, and reviewable elicitation.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Expose permitted Shipkit resources and operations through MCP without treating the protocol as lifecycle authority or a policy boundary.
systems.limitation: The reviewed elicitation page is a draft; MCP servers must not request secrets through form mode, and protocol compliance does not make a tool trustworthy.
systems.falsificationTest: An MCP client must be unable to perform any action rejected by the local CLI for identical cycle, scope, risk, and approval inputs.

systems.name: Agent2Agent Protocol
systems.mechanism: Capability discovery and transport for opaque independent agents, long-running tasks, streaming, and resumable interactions.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Keep internal APIs transport-neutral and add A2A only after at least one real execution adapter and an interoperability use case exist.
systems.limitation: A2A transports agent work but does not define Shipkit authorization, evidence acceptance, repository truth, or outcome evaluation.
systems.falsificationTest: Adopt only if two independent agent clients need the protocol and both preserve identical Shipkit policy and cycle semantics.

systems.name: Open Policy Agent
systems.mechanism: Policy decision and enforcement separation, versioned bundles, decision identifiers, trace correlation, and sensitive-field masking.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Keep the dependency-free local policy engine and copy the decision-record shape; add Rego only when shared external policy packs show value.
systems.limitation: OPA supplies a general policy engine, not Shipkit's domain model, action enforcement, or product-outcome judgment.
systems.falsificationTest: A future OPA adapter must match every local policy fixture and remain optional when the external service is unavailable.

systems.name: SLSA
systems.mechanism: Versioned provenance and verification-summary formats with incremental build and source assurance tracks.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Adapt subjects, materials, provenance, and verification statements to evolution-cycle claims without claiming formal SLSA conformance.
systems.limitation: Supply-chain provenance can prove lineage and controls but cannot prove correctness, safety, or user value.
systems.falsificationTest: An independent verifier must reject changed subjects, missing materials, and unsupported assurance elevation.

systems.name: OpenTelemetry
systems.mechanism: Vendor-neutral traces, metrics, logs, and semantic conventions for correlation across runtimes.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Export optional telemetry from authoritative Shipkit records; telemetry must never become the cycle journal.
systems.limitation: Generative-AI semantic conventions have moved repositories and include development-status fields, so pinning and compatibility tests are required.
systems.falsificationTest: Disabling every telemetry exporter must leave cycle correctness, recovery, and evidence verification unchanged.

systems.name: OpenAI Responses web search
systems.mechanism: Agentic search exposes search actions, inline URL annotations, a complete source list, domain controls, live-access controls, and token budgets.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Adapt these provider-neutral record fields before choosing a paid search provider; preserve complete sources and query trajectory in Shipkit evidence.
systems.limitation: The API requires credentials and incurs provider cost; first-party benchmark claims are not independent evidence of Shipkit user value.
systems.falsificationTest: A provider-neutral fixture must preserve queries, actions, complete sources, exact citations, budget, and stop reason without importing provider objects into kernel state.

systems.name: BrowseComp
systems.mechanism: Hard-to-find but easy-to-verify questions test persistent, strategic browsing with inexpensive short-answer grading.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Adapt verification asymmetry, trajectory retention, and budget reporting for research tests rather than copying the task distribution.
systems.limitation: Short stable answers do not represent open-ended user research, and the published deep-research model was trained for BrowseComp-like tasks.
systems.falsificationTest: A Shipkit research fixture must measure retrieval, citation support, contradiction handling, cost, and decision usefulness separately.

systems.name: SWE-bench benchmark family
systems.mechanism: Containerized repository tasks and hidden regression tests provide useful but narrow coding-agent evaluation.
systems.shipkitBoundary: Use curated repository tasks only as one technical slice alongside policy, recovery, cost, product outcome, and learning measures.
systems.limitation: Audits reported material task-design problems and contamination in SWE-bench Verified, then estimated roughly thirty percent broken tasks in SWE-Bench Pro.
systems.falsificationTest: No aggregate score may gate Shipkit promotion until sampled tasks pass independent prompt, test, environment, and contamination review.

contradictions.claim: Shipkit can differentiate primarily as an agent control plane.
contradictions.impact: OpenHands now markets a broad agent control plane with policy, audit, budgeting, sandboxing, and optimization, while mature projects own policy and telemetry primitives.
contradictions.resolution: Differentiate through cross-agent decision-to-outcome evidence and measured later-cycle learning; avoid generic control-plane positioning.

contradictions.claim: A larger custom durable runtime is a defensible near-term advantage.
contradictions.impact: Durability, replay, interrupts, and pluggable workflow engines are already mature adjacent capabilities.
contradictions.resolution: Keep the portable local kernel and integrate distributed engines only for demonstrated use cases.

contradictions.claim: A leading public coding benchmark score is durable proof of product quality.
contradictions.impact: Task flaws, contamination, narrow distributions, and setup sensitivity can dominate aggregate scores.
contradictions.resolution: Use multi-dimensional, versioned, sampled, independently reviewed evaluations tied to Shipkit user outcomes.

contradictions.claim: MCP or A2A adoption supplies authorization and trustworthy agent behavior.
contradictions.impact: Protocols transport requests and context; enforcement behavior, timeouts, secret handling, and trust remain implementation responsibilities.
contradictions.resolution: Keep Shipkit policy and evidence acceptance transport-neutral and test protocol adapters for parity.
